item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found myself hanging on every word. It's simultaneously simplistic and brutal. I don't really care that we know next to nothing about the anomaly or the format screw or whatever. It's an experience and I'm grateful I had it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are things that I like about this and things that I don't like, but I'm giving it a +1 because I'm glad it exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still allowed to rate an article based on its content, whatever that reason may be. The fact that I think it shows a bad example of what articles on the mainlist should look like is plenty enough reason for me. Also the quip about 100 points was of course an example, not some actual arbitrary limit that I set for this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read it as the SCP database is it's own containment by choice. I think that would mix it with the rest of the SCP stuff nicely.

I think it's better to feel than to understand. What is 3183? What is dissociation? Everyone has their own answer.

PS:Think about 3999,3005,Allison Eckhart and then think about This Question : What is "mate"？Ask yourself and read this article again.To feel:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're really supposed to vote based on the content of the page, not to keep it below an arbitrary vote threshold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1 on this. I like the concept and visuals a lot, but upvoting this would be like advocating for copy cats to make similar seemingly pointless format screws with seemingly meatless writing.

I'm not claiming that this skip lacks in writing and that the format screw is pointless. But to many a writer it will probably seem so. I can easily imagine some hopeful new author reading through skips and seeing something like this with a 100+ score, thinking 'gee, the SCP guyses really seem to enjoy their cooky formatting and obtuse manner of stating facts. Imma spam the mainlist with exactly that kind of articles!'

I don't want there to be another 3183, so to say.

item.title: way to much of a format break
item.content:encoded: It just ruins it for me, sorry -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Who gave Archy and Mehitabel to the Foundation!?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for finalizing this skip as a printout of the mind of Bill Wurtz.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote. -1

But though there is not really anything anomalous about it, i really like how the execution work out well and the format you used. nice job

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking back on this, I've decided to Novote it instead. I've come back to this because I felt like maybe it wasn't as bad as I thought it was. Heck, this thing is alright in my book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was going to downvote on the grounds of it not making sense without the explanation provided in the comments.

Then I realized that my personal philosophy has always been that the title of a work must be read as a part of the work, and you literally called it "dissociation" so it's my own darn fault if I can't figure it out.

Then I was going to downvote on the grounds that I don't much care for the idea of containing something that's part of the human experience. I experience dissociation sometimes when my panic attacks get bad, and it feels kind of cheap to me to just throw this in as "Now it's contained" as if that's something that can be handwaved away. And besides, while dissociation is clearly a lot of things that are strange and not good, it's certainly not ANOMALOUS, in that it seems to follow something resembling scientific laws and is part of reality, it doesn't 'break any rules' so to speak.

But then I figured that it would be unfair of me to judge this on the grounds of "What I think an SCP should be." I don't LIKE the idea of a work that treats mental illness as something the foundation can contain, but if the work exists, and it's done well, then it's done well. And I don't LIKE the idea of the foundation containing things I don't think are anomalous, but then that's just my opinion and there is no canon.

So +1. It's not something I would have written, and not even something I like, but I believe that quality lies in execution and not in concept, and I can't imagine it being better executed, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still think this would have a better effect under a different format than SCP, but I'm changing my -1 to a +1 after re reading this for the fifth time and deciding I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, okay, I love this. It's poetic in a way I very rarely see on the site, and absolutely drew me in. Strong upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: no-vote, this is really cool but I need to think if I should upvote it or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm obviously a huge fan of format screws, so neither the idea (dissociation) or the audacity of the presentation are turning me off. It's everything else. The writing feels aggressively purple to me (I know it's supposed to, I know it's all on purpose, I just don't care for it), the concepts mentioned seem random (why is Freud in there in the middle? what's the colored text doing other than being colorful?) and there's the fact that it doesn't really add anything to the concept it's presenting. Yes, it's a description of dissociation. Are we containing dissociation? Are we containing the metaphysical concept of a place where you can dissociate to? If I dissociate, is this where I am? Is there something to this other than a description of something that already happens? By containing this, are we somehow affecting the concept or act of dissociation for others?

Put it like this: In the Foundation universe, does the containment of SCP-3183 affect anything? Is anything better or worse? I feel like that's not explored, and therefore this is less of a Foundation-universe phenomenon than it is…well, concrete poetry. Which is fine. I used to publish that in a literary magazine. I would have loved this. But it doesn't fit the Foundation for me. Downvoted for that reason primarily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took two read-throughs, but I think I really really like this article. It hits a very personal, raw place inside me, and it will stay with me for some time.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing this sentiment. I wish there there was more here, but as it stands I like its current content, the mood it evokes, and I also just enjoy seeing people push the boundaries of what a mainlister can be.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There isn't quite enough here, which I rarely say these days. Mostly that's an issue with the very end — I think there's the right amount of content up until the three 'hellos'. I wanted more, and not in the "leave them wanting more" sense. At least for me.

However, I still like this, so I'm still upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dissociation (psychology definition)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's the concept of dissociation, ripped from the collective minds of humanity and left to stew and pout in a database entry. It was our self-doubts and personal fears and low self-esteem and imposter syndrome and our disconnect from reality. Now? It's just a thing, pouting in a conceptual cell.

It has written it's own entry because the actual entry is the prison for the concept.

I think all the clues are there, you just have to put the puzzle pieces you're given together and extrapolate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What exactly is this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was on the fence about this for a while- I adore the format screw, but the lack of clarity made me a bit hesitant to upvote. But I think wanting skips to be super clear all the time it a bad habit that stifles creativity and innovation, and I really really like the screw. This works on its own regardless.

So, +1. great job :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Abstract or not, this doesn't qualify for me as an SCP at all. There isn't even any implications for what the skip is supposed to be.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My main problems with this are

  • as Lucio has said, it seems like format breaking just for the sake of it
  • there wasn't anything here that did anything for me, sorry.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't mind abstract story lines, but there at least has to be a story line to begin with. It feels empty, with no real meat to bite into. I don't know if I'm just dumb, but I just couldn't see the story here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, this is way too abstract for me, and it feels as if it's just format breaking for the sake of format breaking.

Others seem to like this, but I legitimately don't see the point.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spacing, capitalization, title, etc. Are all completely off. I went through the article and fixed everything.

jk

I may be a bit too thick to read between the lines and really see what the anomaly is. Though, whatever it may be, "self-contained/penning its own entry" is sufficient justification for such a format break in my eyes.

Beyond that, it was certainly entertaining, and stylistically satisfying, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see where you are going with this, and in a way i like the idea. It's quite original, and i appreciate your attempt to make something different.

…But at the same time it feels like you're trying a bit too hard to break the format, it's like you wanted to make it abstract for the sake of it being abstract.

I don't want to downvote this since i believe it has some potential, so no-vote for now.

Edit : Eh you know what, i think something clicked. I like it now. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what, sure. I wanted so badly to hate this, but by the end I realised I didn't, and it's sure-as-hell creative.

Tentative upvote, at least for the sake of encouraging people to expand away from the format.

See later comment. I like this much more now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get what you're going for, but this feels like it's trying too hard to break format for the sake of breaking format

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you will get plenty of downvotes for this, but I think it's great.
the feeling of dissociation is one i am too familiar with, and this does well at capturing it.
i look forward to more of your stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh hi

thanks for checking in

i'm

still a piece of garbage

In all seriousness, I like the idea, but I couldn't stop reading in a bill wurtz voice. In addition, I feel there just isn't enough and it's a bit abstract for my tastes. Novote from me.

Edit: I decided to come back to this and analyze it more thoroughly, especially with a new mindset and perspective. Changing my no-vote to an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thanks to kinchtheknifeblade , keyii , feline on irc, saintjimmy on irc, cyrusfiredawn on irc, and bunton on irc.

sorry if this ends up biting it. theres not really many good rules to follow.

still follows the format even though i wanted to break it over my knee. its coded.

its dissociation.

i figure this was the most logical way for dissociation to communicate.

alternate title is "running away with what freud said" because i had that on loop the entire time.
